1.pericope_label: YHWH’s Faithful Remembrance of His Covenant
1.synthesis: Psalm 105 is Israel’s great covenant-history doxology, retelling the story of Abraham through the Exodus as a sustained act of YHWH’s faithfulness to his sworn word. Its opening verses (vv. 1–7) are quoted in 1 Chronicles 16:8–22 as the song David appointed at the ark’s return to Jerusalem. Covenant memory (zikru, “remember his wonderful works,” v. 5) is active appropriation, not nostalgia. Remarkably, the patriarchs are reinterpreted as “his anointed ones” and “his prophets” (v. 15), a designation Genesis never assigns them. The Joseph narrative is compressed into a theology of suffering and vindication: “his feet were hurt with fetters; his neck was put in a collar of iron” (barzel ba’ah nafsho—“iron entered his soul,” v. 18). Crucially, “the Lord sent Joseph” (v. 17) before Pharaoh sent to release him (v. 20)—YHWH’s sending precedes every human chain of events. The psalm closes with the purpose clause for the entire covenant history: “that they might keep his statutes and observe his laws” (v. 45).
1.historical_context: Psalm 105 and its companion Psalm 106 form a diptych: 105 tells the story from YHWH’s faithful side, 106 from Israel’s unfaithful side. The designation of the patriarchs as “his anointed ones and prophets” (v. 15) reflects post-Mosaic theological reflection on the whole sweep of covenant history. The plague order in Psalm 105 (vv. 28–36) differs from the sequence in Exodus 7–12, suggesting liturgical rather than strictly chronological memory. The barzel ba’ah nafsho phrase (v. 18) is ambiguous in Hebrew—literally “iron came into his soul”—and may indicate either iron fetters around his neck or the spiritual agony of his suffering, or both simultaneously.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 105 is organized around YHWH’s advance preparation through each generation: from the Abrahamic covenant (vv. 8–15) to Joseph sent ahead of the family (vv. 16–22) to the Egyptian plagues sent ahead of the Exodus (vv. 23–38) to the provision for the wilderness (vv. 39–41) to the gift of Canaan (vv. 42–45). The verb “sent” (shalach) is the hinge: YHWH sent Joseph (v. 17), sent Moses and Aaron (v. 26), sent darkness (v. 28), sent plagues (v. 29), and finally sent Israel out with silver and gold (v. 37). The psalm is a theology of divine sending that precedes and determines human history.
1.theological_application: Psalm 105 grounds Israel’s identity in YHWH’s zikru—his active memory of the covenant he swore to Abraham (vv. 8–10). YHWH’s memory of his covenant is the theological engine that drives history forward. The psalm therefore calls Israel to the same active memory (v. 5) as the appropriate response: remembering YHWH’s works is not mere historical recollection but covenant participation. The purpose clause of verse 45 also reframes the Exodus: deliverance from Egypt was not an end in itself but the means to a people formed to keep Torah. Election is always election for purpose.
1.christology: The Joseph narrative in Psalm 105 is the Old Testament’s most developed type of Christ: “the Lord sent Joseph” (v. 17) before anyone else did, just as “God sent his Son” (Gal 4:4) before any human arrangement could account for it. The iron that entered Joseph’s soul (v. 18) before his vindication prefigures the suffering of the one who was “obedient to the point of death” (Phil 2:8). And the purpose clause of Psalm 105:45—that the delivered people might keep YHWH’s statutes—finds its new covenant fulfillment in Christ writing the law on the heart (Jer 31:33; Heb 10:16).
